claim: Top models in general benchmarks (MMLU, MATH) often do not rank highest on agentic/tool-calling benchmarks; domain-specific evals are required for agent selection.
actionable: Don't use general LLM benchmarks to select models for agents; create or use domain-specific agentic eval suites (agent leaderboard, TauBench) that test tool-calling and multi-turn reasoning.
source_title: Ranking Agentic LLMs — Pratik Bhavsar, Galileo

claim: Reasoning models (O series) perform poorly at multi-tool-call tasks; they output only single tool calls even when multiple are required, making them unfit for agents despite high cost.
actionable: Avoid reasoning models for agent work involving multiple sequential or parallel tool calls; they often fail to output all required calls and carry higher costs.
source_title: Ranking Agentic LLMs — Pratik Bhavsar, Galileo

claim: Smaller, cheaper models often deliver similar agentic performance as large flagship models with small enough gaps that cost-performance is decisively in their favor.
actionable: Evaluate models on your agent task; don't assume the largest/most expensive model is optimal; test mid-tier and smaller models for cost-performance tradeoffs.
source_title: Ranking Agentic LLMs — Pratik Bhavsar, Galileo

claim: LLM-as-judge metrics require extensive calibration, multiple judges, and prompt optimization; uncalibrated LLM judges can have high correlation errors with human judgment.
actionable: If using LLM-as-judge metrics, run correlation studies against annotated data first; use multiple judges, employ self-consistency voting, and validate on small annotated datasets.
source_title: Ranking Agentic LLMs — Pratik Bhavsar, Galileo

claim: Domain-specific benchmarks (e.g., TauBench with airline/retail domains) are more predictive of real-world agent performance than generic multi-domain evaluations.
actionable: Develop or source domain-specific agentic evals that match your use case (healthcare, finance, etc.); generic benchmarks miss domain-specific failure modes.
source_title: Ranking Agentic LLMs — Pratik Bhavsar, Galileo

claim: Action completion metrics (task fully accomplished vs. tool selection quality) matter more for production evals; focus on whether the agent completed all requested actions, not just tool accuracy.
actionable: When building agent evals, prioritize action completion metrics over tool selection; measure whether the agent accomplished all user goals, not just correct tool calls.
source_title: Ranking Agentic LLMs — Pratik Bhavsar, Galileo

claim: Benchmarks saturate near 0.95 score, losing discriminative power; harder, more realistic multi-turn scenarios with complex goals are needed for valid future evals.
actionable: When designing benchmarks, ensure they don't saturate above 0.9; use realistic multi-turn scenarios with 5-7 goals per task to maintain discrimination between models.
source_title: Ranking Agentic LLMs — Pratik Bhavsar, Galileo

claim: Cost awareness is critical in agentic evals; models spending 2x tokens for marginally better performance can be deceptive without cost-adjusted scoring.
actionable: In your agent evals, track and report cost per successful completion alongside accuracy; normalize scores by token usage to avoid cost-blind decisions.
source_title: Ranking Agentic LLMs — Pratik Bhavsar, Galileo
